title: Dundee cake
subtitle: The Scottish fruit cake with a blanched almond crown
excerpt: A round Scottish fruit cake made by the creaming method: 225 g of plain flour, 175 g of butter, mixed dried fruit, mixed peel, and a top covered in concentric circles of blanched almonds before baking. A slow bake at 160°C. Keeps for 2 weeks.
sourceNotes: The Dundee cake is a Scottish fruit cake documented in Florence White, Good Things in England (1932): White notes the Dundee cake is distinguished from other Scottish fruit cakes by the whole blanched almond decoration on the top and the absence of excessive spice. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: the chapter on rich cakes includes several Scottish-style fruit cake variants using the creaming method with dried fruit and peel. Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg: Acton documents the approach of adding dried fruit after the batter is combined rather than creaming it in, which preserves the fruit's shape — the same technique used here.

recipe.freezeNotes: Wrap the completely cooled cake in two layers of cling film and freeze for up to 3 months. Defrost at room temperature overnight.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Improves significantly with keeping. Wrap in baking paper and foil; store at room temperature for up to 2 weeks. Matures and the almond flavour deepens.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: conventional oven, middle shelf — fan oven risks drying the surface before the centre is cooked

recipeTools.notes: 20 cm round tin, greased and double-lined (cake needs a long bake; the double lining protects the outside).

glossaryTerms.definition: Lining a cake tin with two layers of baking paper on the base and sides, and wrapping the outside of the tin with a double layer of brown paper or newspaper tied with kitchen twine. The outer wrapping insulates the cake from the direct heat of the oven and prevents the outside from overbaking before the centre is cooked — essential for large, dense fruit cakes that bake for 90 minutes or more.

content.text: The Dundee cake is identified by two things: the blanched almonds arranged in concentric circles across the top, and the absence of the heavy dark spicing that many other fruit cakes use. The fruit mix here is lighter : sultanas, currants, and glacé cherries and the almond comes from ground almonds in the batter rather than marzipan. The result is a gentler fruit cake that doesn't need to be made months ahead, though it does improve considerably with even a week's keeping.

A conventional (non-fan) oven is the right choice here. A long bake at 160°C in fan produces a surface that sets and dries before the centre is fully cooked; in a conventional oven the heat is gentler and more even.
the tin is the standard protection for a 100-minute bake.

items.prepNote: halved and rinsed to remove the syrup
blanched whole almonds for the top

content.text: Heat the oven to 160°C conventional. Grease a 20 cm round tin and line the base and sides with a double layer of baking paper. Cut a strip of brown paper or doubled newspaper to wrap around the outside of the tin; secure with kitchen twine. The outer wrapping insulates the tin through the long bake.
Cream butter and sugar, add eggs

Beat the {{unsalted-butter}} softened butter and {{caster-sugar}} caster sugar until pale and fluffy, about 4 minutes. Add the {{eggs}} eggs one at a time, beating well after each. If the mixture looks as though it's about to curdle, add a tablespoon of the flour before the next egg. Beat in the {{almond-extract}} almond extract.
Fold in dry ingredients and fruit

Sift together the {{plain-flour}}, {{almond-flour}}, {{baking-powder}}, and {{mixed-spice}}. Fold into the batter in two stages. Toss the {{sultanas}}, {{currants}}, {{cherries-glace}} halved glacé cherries, and {{mixed-peel}} in a tablespoon of flour (this helps prevent them sinking to the bottom), then fold into the batter.
Fill the tin and arrange almonds

Spoon the batter into the prepared tin and smooth the top. Arrange the blanched {{almonds-flaked}} almonds across the surface in concentric circles, starting at the outside edge and working inward. Press them lightly so they sit level. Leave a small cluster in the centre.

Bake for 95–105 minutes. The cake is done when a skewer through the centre comes out clean and the top is a deep golden-brown with the almonds a light toast colour. Do not open the oven before 80 minutes. Leave to cool completely in the tin before removing — at least 2 hours. Wrap well and store at room temperature.
